1:

On "I will write upon these tables the words that were in the first tables," the reformed Calvin and witnesses note the writing. On "which thou brakest," the wesleyan-arminian Wesley and grammatical-historical note the breach: "which thou brakest" — the reminder of the breach (the first tables broken, the covenant broken by the calf); the providence (the law renewed after the apostasy, the figure of grace restoring the broken covenant; the patience of God renewing his word to the people who had broken it). The verse displays the new tables and the renewal of the law.

Clarke and Gill note that "keeping mercy for thousands, forgiving iniquity and transgression and sin" — God keeps mercy for thousands (of generations, or persons), forgiving every kind of sin (iniquity, transgression, sin); the abounding mercy (the vast, generous forgiveness, the mercy for thousands far outweighing the visiting to the third and fourth generation, Exodus 20:5-6); the forgiveness (the pardon of all manner of sin).

On "forgiving iniquity and transgression and sin," the reformed and witnesses note the forgiveness. Gill notes the forgiving of all manner of sin (iniquity, transgression, sin, the three words covering every kind) — the full forgiveness (God's readiness to pardon, the ground of hope; the mercy magnified).

On "and that will by no means clear the guilty," interpreters expound the justice. The reformed and the dominant reading note the justice — "that will by no means clear the guilty": God, though merciful, will by no means clear (acquit, leave unpunished) the guilty (the impenitent, the obstinate); the witnesses note the lesson — the holding together of mercy and justice (God merciful, yet just; pardoning the penitent, yet not clearing the guilty; his justice not set aside by his mercy); the great question — how God can be both "merciful... forgiving iniquity" and yet "by no means clear the guilty" — answered fully in the cross of Christ, where mercy and justice meet (Romans 3:25-26, "that he might be just, and the justifier of him which believeth in Jesus"; the guilt punished in Christ, the believer pardoned; God just and the justifier); "visiting the iniquity of the fathers" — the justice that visits sin (the consequences of sin descending, the warning, Exodus 20:5).
